sections.plain_summary: Functional dyspepsia (FD) is a chronic digestive condition causing persistent upper abdominal discomfort, pain, bloating, early fullness, or nausea — without any structural cause found on endoscopy or imaging. It is one of the most common functional gastrointestinal disorders, yet it remains poorly understood and is often inadequately treated in mainstream clinical settings.
sections.detailed_summary:
Functional dyspepsia is defined by bothersome symptoms originating in the upper gastrointestinal tract — particularly the stomach and duodenum — that persist for at least three months with onset at least six months prior, and that cannot be explained by structural abnormalities such as ulcers, tumours, or visible inflammation. The Rome IV diagnostic criteria formally divide it into two overlapping subtypes: postprandial distress syndrome (PDS), dominated by meal-triggered fullness and early satiation, and epigastric pain syndrome (EPS), characterised by pain or burning centred in the upper abdomen that may be unrelated to eating.

Despite the absence of visible pathology, FD is not imaginary or purely psychological. Research has identified several plausible biological mechanisms: impaired gastric accommodation (the stomach's inability to relax properly after a meal), delayed gastric emptying, duodenal hypersensitivity to acid and fat, low-grade duodenal inflammation involving eosinophils and mast cells, disruption of the gut-brain axis, altered gut microbiota, and heightened visceral pain perception. Post-infectious FD — arising after a bout of acute gastroenteritis — is a well-documented subtype, suggesting that gut infection can trigger lasting changes in nerve sensitivity and mucosal immune activity.

H. pylori infection is an important and treatable contributor in a subset of people; eradication can provide lasting symptom relief in roughly 10–15% of those infected, making testing a standard first step. Beyond that, management is often trial-and-error. Dietary modifications, acid suppression, prokinetic drugs, low-dose antidepressants targeting gut-brain signalling, and psychological therapies each help some people but not others. Many individuals spend years cycling through treatments without identifying their personal drivers.

Self-directed investigation — food and symptom journalling, trialling dietary changes methodically, researching the literature, and seeking out practitioners who investigate root causes rather than only managing symptoms — is a legitimate and often necessary part of living with FD. Finding a gastroenterologist willing to go beyond endoscopy-and-PPI, or a functional medicine clinician who investigates motility, microbiome, and mucosal immune function, can make a meaningful difference.

prevalence.summary: Functional dyspepsia affects roughly one in eight people globally, making it one of the most common functional GI disorders.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary widely depending on diagnostic criteria used (Rome III vs Rome IV) and how 'functional dyspepsia' is separated from overlapping conditions like IBS and GERD. Rome IV criteria are more restrictive than older definitions, so prevalence may appear lower in newer studies. FD is more commonly diagnosed in women, and it disproportionately affects people in younger to middle adult age groups, though it occurs across all ages. Many people with FD never receive a formal diagnosis.

subgroups.name: Postprandial Distress Syndrome (PDS)
subgroups.description: The meal-triggered subtype of FD, characterised by symptoms that arise after eating even normal-sized meals. It is the more common of the two Rome IV subtypes.
distinguishing_features: Bothersome postprandial fullness occurring after ordinary meals
Early satiation that prevents finishing a normal-sized meal
Symptoms reliably worsened by eating, particularly fatty or large meals
Upper abdominal bloating and nausea are common
Often associated with impaired gastric accommodation — the stomach fails to relax to receive food
May overlap significantly with gastroparesis

subgroups.name: Epigastric Pain Syndrome (EPS)
subgroups.description: The pain-dominant subtype of FD, characterised by epigastric pain or burning that may not be consistently related to meals.
distinguishing_features: Intermittent pain or burning localised to the epigastric region (upper centre of the abdomen)
Pain may be relieved or worsened by eating — relationship to food is variable
Not relieved by defecation (distinguishes it from IBS-related pain)
Pain can be severe enough to disrupt daily activity
May respond better to acid suppression than PDS
Can be confused with peptic ulcer disease or GERD

subgroups.name: Post-infectious FD
subgroups.description: A subtype that develops after an episode of acute infectious gastroenteritis, with no prior history of dyspepsia.
distinguishing_features: Clear temporal link — symptoms begin in the weeks following a bout of food poisoning, traveller's diarrhoea, or viral gastroenteritis
Duodenal immune activation (mast cells, eosinophils) often found on biopsy
May be associated with impaired gastric motility as a result of enteric nerve damage
Can partially resemble post-infectious IBS but with predominantly upper GI symptoms
Prognosis may differ — some people recover more fully over time than those with idiopathic FD

subgroups.name: H. pylori-associated FD
subgroups.description: FD in which Helicobacter pylori infection is present and eradication leads to sustained symptom improvement.
distinguishing_features: Active H. pylori infection confirmed on testing
Symptoms may improve meaningfully following successful antibiotic eradication
Some guidelines now classify post-eradication, symptom-free patients as 'cured' rather than FD
Represents the only clearly reversible infectious driver in FD
H. pylori does not cause FD in everyone infected — most remain asymptomatic

subgroups.name: Overlap FD–IBS
subgroups.description: A significant minority of people meet diagnostic criteria for both functional dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome simultaneously.
distinguishing_features: Both upper GI (epigastric pain, nausea, early fullness) and lower GI symptoms (altered bowel habit, bloating, cramping) present
Higher overall symptom burden and worse quality of life than either condition alone
Suggests shared pathophysiology — possibly involving central sensitisation and gut-brain axis dysregulation across the whole GI tract
May respond to treatments targeting both upper and lower GI function
More commonly seen in women and those with a history of anxiety or early-life adversity

symptoms.notes: A heavy, lingering sensation of fullness after eating that persists well beyond what would be expected given the meal size. Even modest meals can trigger significant discomfort.
Feeling uncomfortably full after only a few bites, making it difficult or impossible to finish a normal-sized meal. Often linked to impaired gastric accommodation.
Pain or aching centred in the upper abdomen, between the navel and breastbone. May occur between meals or be triggered by eating. Can vary from dull to sharp.
A burning sensation in the upper abdomen distinct from heartburn (which is felt in the chest). May be acid-related or a result of visceral hypersensitivity.
Often meal-related but can be present at other times. Rarely leads to vomiting in FD without another cause. Persistent nausea is one of the most disabling symptoms.
A sensation of pressure, tightness, or distension in the upper abdomen. Distinct from lower abdominal bloating more typical of IBS.
Frequent or bothersome burping, which can be supragastric (air swallowing driven by discomfort) or a result of abnormal gastric gas handling.
Reduced desire to eat, often because eating triggers or worsens symptoms. Over time this can lead to unintentional weight loss.
Can occur as a result of food avoidance. Significant or rapid unexplained weight loss warrants investigation to exclude organic disease.
Overlap with GERD is common. Presence of heartburn does not exclude FD but warrants separate evaluation.
Chronic fatigue is common in FD, partly due to disrupted sleep from nocturnal symptoms and partly due to the broader systemic burden of chronic pain and poor nutrition.
The gut-brain axis runs both ways — GI distress drives anxiety, and anxiety amplifies gut symptoms. These are mechanistically linked, not simply a psychological response to illness.
Nocturnal epigastric pain or discomfort can interrupt sleep. Poor sleep in turn lowers pain thresholds and worsens gut motility.

key_patterns: Symptoms meeting Rome IV criteria: bothersome postprandial fullness, early satiation, epigastric pain, or epigastric burning — present for at least 3 months with onset at least 6 months prior
Normal upper endoscopy (oesophagogastroduodenoscopy) — absence of peptic ulcer, oesophagitis, malignancy, or other structural pathology
H. pylori testing positive — an important and actionable finding even when structural pathology is absent
Gastric emptying study showing delayed emptying — present in a subset of FD patients, suggesting overlap with gastroparesis
Duodenal eosinophilia on biopsy — emerging finding in some FD patients, particularly post-infectious cases
Symptom pattern worsened consistently by fat-rich or large meals, suggesting impaired gastric accommodation
Symptom onset traceable to an acute gastrointestinal infection — a key history finding for post-infectious FD

at_home.name: Detailed food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Keeping a structured daily log of everything eaten (including portion size, fat content, meal timing), alongside symptom type, severity, and timing relative to meals. Running this for at least 2–4 weeks can reveal patterns — for example, whether high-fat meals, large portions, or specific foods (onions, wheat, coffee, alcohol, spicy food) consistently trigger symptoms.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a low-risk information-gathering exercise. Those already restricting food significantly should be cautious about further elimination without professional guidance, as nutritional adequacy can be affected.

at_home.name: Meal size and composition experiment
at_home.description: Systematically trialling smaller, more frequent meals with reduced fat content for 1–2 weeks to assess whether symptom burden changes. This targets impaired gastric accommodation — a common driver in PDS.
at_home.safety_notes: Ensure caloric and nutritional adequacy is maintained. If symptoms worsen significantly or new symptoms emerge, stop and seek clinical assessment.

at_home.name: Low-FODMAP trial (short-term)
at_home.description: A structured 2–6 week elimination of fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAPs), commonly used in IBS, may also benefit some people with FD given the significant overlap between these conditions. Symptoms are tracked during elimination and then foods are reintroduced one category at a time.
at_home.safety_notes: The low-FODMAP diet is nutritionally restrictive and is best done with guidance from a FODMAP-trained dietitian. Long-term restriction without reintroduction is not recommended and can affect gut microbiota diversity.

at_home.name: Caffeine and alcohol elimination
at_home.description: Removing coffee, tea, energy drinks, and alcohol completely for 2–4 weeks while tracking symptoms. Both caffeine and alcohol stimulate acid secretion and can impair gastric motility.
at_home.safety_notes: Abrupt caffeine withdrawal causes transient headaches for several days — this is normal and not a gut symptom. Gradual reduction may be preferable.

clinical.name: Upper endoscopy (OGD / EGD)
clinical.description: Direct visualisation of the oesophagus, stomach, and duodenum to rule out peptic ulcer disease, erosive oesophagitis, gastric malignancy, coeliac disease (via duodenal biopsy), and other structural pathology. In FD, results are by definition normal or near-normal. Biopsies of the duodenum may be taken to assess for eosinophilia or coeliac disease.
clinical.availability: Ordered by a gastroenterologist. Generally performed under light sedation in an endoscopy unit. May require a referral from a GP or primary care physician.

clinical.name: H. pylori testing
clinical.description: Can be performed non-invasively via urea breath test (UBT) or stool antigen test, or via biopsy taken during endoscopy (rapid urease test, histology, culture). All non-invasive tests require stopping proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) for at least 2 weeks beforehand for accurate results.
clinical.availability: Urea breath test and stool antigen test are widely available through GPs and gastroenterologists. Serology (blood antibody test) is less accurate and does not distinguish active from past infection.

clinical.name: Gastric emptying scintigraphy
clinical.description: The standard test for gastroparesis, performed by having the patient eat a radiolabelled solid meal (typically egg) and imaging gastric emptying over 4 hours. Delayed emptying is found in a meaningful subset of FD patients and changes management. Normal emptying does not exclude FD.
clinical.availability: Available at most teaching hospitals and larger gastroenterology centres. Requires specialist referral. Not universally ordered in routine FD workup — may need to be specifically requested.

clinical.name: Duodenal biopsy with eosinophil count
clinical.description: Biopsies taken from the duodenum during endoscopy are assessed for elevated eosinophil counts and mast cell infiltration. Duodenal eosinophilia has been identified in FD patients, particularly post-infectious cases, and may guide treatment with mast-cell-stabilising or anti-eosinophilic therapies.
clinical.availability: Available at centres where pathologists are familiar with GI eosinophil counting. Not yet a standard part of routine FD workup — requires a gastroenterologist who is aware of the research and specifically requests eosinophil quantification.

clinical.name: Gastric accommodation test (nutrient drink test / gastric barostat)
clinical.description: Assesses the stomach's ability to relax and expand to receive a meal. The nutrient drink test involves gradually drinking a standardised liquid meal until fullness is reached — impaired accommodation correlates with early satiation. The gastric barostat is a research tool using an intragastric balloon.
clinical.availability: The nutrient drink test is used in specialist centres and some academic gastroenterology units. The barostat is largely research-only. Not routinely available in standard clinical practice.

clinical.name: Coeliac disease serology
clinical.description: Blood tests including anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) and total IgA, used to screen for coeliac disease — which can present with upper GI symptoms mimicking FD. Positive serology is confirmed by duodenal biopsy.
clinical.availability: Widely available through GPs and gastroenterologists. Must be performed while the patient is eating gluten (not already on a gluten-free diet) to be meaningful.

clinical.name: Abdominal ultrasound
clinical.description: Used to exclude gallstones, biliary pathology, and pancreatic abnormalities that may cause upper abdominal pain mimicking FD. Generally one of the first-line investigations ordered alongside blood tests.
clinical.availability: Widely available through radiology departments. Ordered by GP or specialist.

clinical.name: Hydrogen breath testing
clinical.description: Can assess for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) and carbohydrate malabsorption (lactose, fructose), which overlap significantly with FD and may contribute to upper GI symptoms in some people.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterology departments and some specialist functional medicine clinics. Home breath test kits are also commercially available, though clinical interpretation is recommended.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: FD and IBS co-occur in 30–40% of cases — far more often than chance. They share overlapping pathophysiology including gut-brain axis dysregulation, visceral hypersensitivity, and altered motility. Many people with FD-IBS overlap experience a higher overall symptom burden and worse quality of life than those with either condition alone.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying confirmed on scintigraphy) and FD-PDS share almost identical symptoms — postprandial fullness, early satiation, nausea, and bloating. The distinction requires formal gastric emptying testing; a meaningful subset of PDS patients have delayed emptying, and the boundary between the two conditions is debated in the literature.

related_conditions.name: H. pylori infection
related_conditions.explanation: Active H. pylori infection can cause or contribute to dyspeptic symptoms, and eradication leads to sustained symptom resolution in approximately 10–15% of infected individuals — a proportion that seems modest but represents a significant number of people. H. pylori testing and treatment is a standard first step in FD investigation.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can produce upper GI symptoms including bloating, early fullness, nausea, and epigastric discomfort that overlap with FD. Impaired gastric motility in FD may predispose to SIBO by slowing the migrating motor complex. Some people with a diagnosis of FD improve with SIBO treatment.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation / visceral hypersensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Heightened sensitivity to normal levels of gastric distension, acid, and fat — mediated by sensitised afferent nerves and altered central pain processing — is a core mechanism in FD, particularly EPS. This explains why symptoms can be severe in the absence of any tissue damage visible on endoscopy.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cell infiltration of the duodenal mucosa has been documented in FD, particularly post-infectious cases. Mast cells release mediators that sensitise enteric nerves and impair smooth muscle function. In some individuals, broader mast cell activation syndrome may be an underlying driver of FD-like symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety is significantly more prevalent in people with FD than in the general population. The relationship is bidirectional — anxiety amplifies gut sensitivity via the gut-brain axis, and persistent GI symptoms drive anxiety. Treating anxiety often improves GI symptoms independently of direct GI treatment.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depressive disorders co-occur with FD at elevated rates. Shared serotonergic pathways likely contribute — the gut contains approximately 95% of the body's serotonin, which regulates both mood and gut motility. Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants target this overlap therapeutically.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: NCGS can produce upper GI symptoms including bloating, nausea, and epigastric discomfort indistinguishable from FD. Some people labelled with FD improve significantly on a gluten-free diet without having coeliac disease. The overlap is difficult to disentangle without systematic elimination and rechallenge.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease can present primarily with upper GI symptoms — nausea, bloating, and postprandial discomfort — without the classic diarrhoea, making it a mimicker of FD. Serology and duodenal biopsy are needed to exclude it, and this is a standard part of the FD workup.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Eosinophilic gastritis and duodenitis can produce symptoms identical to FD. Duodenal eosinophilia has been found in a subset of FD patients, suggesting a mucosal immune mechanism. These conditions may only be identified if biopsies are specifically examined for eosinophil density.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Some people with FD-like symptoms have an underlying sensitivity to dietary histamine, producing upper GI symptoms including nausea, early fullness, and epigastric discomfort after high-histamine foods. The mast-cell involvement documented in FD provides a plausible mechanistic link.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Alterations in the composition and function of the gut microbiome have been described in FD. Dysbiosis may contribute to low-grade mucosal inflammation, altered gut motility, and changes in gut-brain signalling. This is an active area of research with growing evidence.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic nervous system dysfunction — as seen in POTS — can impair gastric motility and accommodation, producing upper GI symptoms that overlap substantially with FD. People with unexplained FD alongside orthostatic symptoms, palpitations, or fatigue may benefit from cardiovascular autonomic evaluation.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / HSD (hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome)
related_conditions.explanation: Gastrointestinal dysmotility, including gastroparesis and functional dyspepsia, is common in hypermobile EDS and HSD, possibly due to autonomic dysfunction and connective tissue abnormalities in the gut wall. FD in the context of joint hypermobility, chronic pain, and dysautonomia warrants consideration of an underlying connective tissue disorder.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can alter gastric motility and produce GI symptoms including nausea, bloating, and altered appetite that overlap with FD. Thyroid function testing is a standard part of excluding organic causes of dyspeptic symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI)
related_conditions.explanation: EPI can cause postprandial bloating, nausea, and upper abdominal discomfort — particularly after fatty meals — that mimics FD. It is underdiagnosed and not always considered in the FD workup. Faecal elastase testing can help exclude significant pancreatic enzyme insufficiency.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Adverse childhood experiences and psychological trauma are significantly overrepresented in people with functional GI disorders including FD. Trauma alters HPA axis function and autonomic tone, which in turn affects gut motility, mucosal immunity, and visceral pain sensitivity. This is not a claim that FD is 'psychological' but that the nervous system changes caused by trauma have real physiological consequences in the gut.

strategies.name: Small, frequent, low-fat meals
strategies.description: Eating 5–6 smaller meals across the day rather than 2–3 large ones reduces the distension load on a stomach with impaired accommodation. Reducing dietary fat is especially relevant because fat slows gastric emptying and triggers the release of cholecystokinin, which further reduces gastric motility. This is often the single most impactful dietary change for those with PDS.
strategies.notes: Nutritional adequacy must be maintained. Some people undershoot calories significantly over time when using this approach. Tracking with a dietitian or nutrition app can help.

strategies.name: H. pylori eradication
strategies.description: For those testing positive for H. pylori, a standard 14-day triple or quadruple antibiotic regimen (with confirmation of eradication via breath or stool test 4+ weeks after completing treatment) offers sustained symptom relief for roughly 10–15% — and is the only FD intervention with a clear curative potential.
strategies.notes: Eradication must be confirmed. Antibiotic resistance is increasing; bismuth quadruple therapy may be preferred in areas with high clarithromycin resistance. Probiotics during and after antibiotics may reduce side effects.

strategies.name: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)
strategies.description: PPIs (omeprazole, lansoprazole, esomeprazole) reduce gastric acid and are most useful for the EPS subtype — particularly epigastric burning and pain. A 4–8 week trial is typically recommended. They are less effective for PDS-predominant symptoms.
strategies.notes: Long-term PPI use has potential downsides including increased risk of SIBO, magnesium deficiency, and altered microbiome composition. They are generally not intended as indefinite treatment for FD. Many people are prescribed them without a clear endpoint.

strategies.name: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs)
strategies.description: Low doses of amitriptyline or nortriptyline (typically 10–50 mg/day — well below antidepressant doses) target gut-brain signalling, reduce visceral pain sensitivity, and modulate gut motility. The DIAMOND trial showed amitriptyline significantly outperformed PPI in functional dyspepsia, particularly EPS.
strategies.notes: Effects on mood at these doses are minimal; the mechanism is neuromodulation of gut afferent pathways. Common side effects include drowsiness, dry mouth, and constipation. Nortriptyline tends to be better tolerated. Should be initiated and monitored by a clinician.

strategies.name: Mirtazapine
strategies.description: A low-dose antidepressant that improves gastric accommodation and reduces nausea. Particularly useful for people with FD-associated weight loss, nausea, and early satiation. Has appetite-stimulating effects.
strategies.notes: Significant sedation and weight gain at higher doses. At low doses (7.5–15 mg) these effects are less marked. Requires prescription and clinician oversight.

strategies.name: Prokinetic medications
strategies.description: Drugs that accelerate gastric emptying (domperidone, metoclopramide, itopride, acotiamide) may help those with PDS and confirmed or suspected delayed gastric emptying. Acotiamide has the strongest evidence base specifically for FD.
strategies.notes: Acotiamide is approved for FD in Japan and some other markets but not widely available in the UK or US. Domperidone carries a small cardiac risk at higher doses. Metoclopramide has CNS side effects with long-term use. Access and availability vary significantly by country.

strategies.name: Gut-directed hypnotherapy
strategies.description: Structured hypnotherapy protocols targeting gut hypersensitivity and the gut-brain axis have evidence for both IBS and FD. Multiple sessions reduce visceral pain sensitivity and improve overall symptom burden in a meaningful proportion of participants.
strategies.notes: Not widely available in standard care. Practitioner quality varies. Audio-based self-guided gut hypnotherapy programmes (such as those developed by Peter Whorwell's group) may offer a more accessible starting point.

strategies.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)
strategies.description: CBT adapted for GI conditions addresses the thought patterns and behaviours (food avoidance, hypervigilance to symptoms, catastrophising) that amplify the gut-brain symptom cycle. Evidence for functional GI disorders broadly is good; FD-specific trials are fewer but supportive.
strategies.notes: Most effective when integrated with physical management rather than offered as a standalone 'it's all in your head' intervention. Patients often resist psychological referral for this reason — framing it as a gut-brain tool rather than a mental health treatment may help.

strategies.name: STW 5 (Iberogast)
strategies.description: A herbal preparation combining nine plant extracts including iberis amara, liquorice, peppermint, and chamomile. Has been studied in multiple randomised controlled trials for FD and IBS, showing significant improvement in symptom scores compared to placebo. Improves gastric accommodation and reduces hypersensitivity.
strategies.notes: Generally well tolerated. Rare hepatotoxicity cases have been reported — a precautionary warning exists in some countries. Should be used at recommended doses and not combined with other potentially hepatotoxic supplements or medications.

strategies.name: Peppermint oil (enteric-coated)
strategies.description: Peppermint oil has antispasmodic effects on smooth muscle and may reduce epigastric pain and discomfort. Enteric-coated capsules release in the small intestine rather than the oesophagus, reducing the risk of heartburn.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger for IBS than FD specifically, but the overlap and similar mechanism make it worth considering. Avoid if heartburn or reflux is a primary symptom — peppermint relaxes the lower oesophageal sphincter.

strategies.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
strategies.description: Structured mindfulness practice reduces autonomic arousal and may lower visceral pain sensitivity over time. Several studies support benefit for functional GI disorders. Daily practice (20–45 minutes) over 8 weeks is the standard protocol.
strategies.notes: Effects are cumulative — benefit requires sustained practice. Some people find that intensive body-focused meditation initially increases awareness of symptoms before it reduces them.

strategies.name: Regular aerobic exercise
strategies.description: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming — 30 minutes, 4–5 times per week) improves gastric motility, reduces anxiety, and lowers systemic inflammation. Several studies show benefit for GI motility disorders.
strategies.notes: High-intensity exercise immediately before or after meals can worsen symptoms in some people. Timing matters — exercise between meals is generally better tolerated.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet
strategies.description: A structured elimination and reintroduction diet that reduces fermentable carbohydrates. Most evidence is in IBS, but given the high FD-IBS overlap and similar mechanisms (fermentation-driven distension, visceral hypersensitivity), a supervised FODMAP trial may benefit some people with FD.
strategies.notes: Must be supervised by a FODMAP-trained dietitian to ensure the reintroduction phase is completed — long-term full restriction is not the goal and may worsen gut microbiome diversity. Results are highly individual.

things_to_avoid: Large meals — even in the absence of trigger foods, meal volume alone can overwhelm impaired gastric accommodation and trigger symptoms
High-fat foods — fatty meals delay gastric emptying and stimulate cholecystokinin release, worsening both fullness and pain
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) — damage the gastric mucosa and can worsen epigastric pain significantly; paracetamol is a safer analgesic alternative
Alcohol — stimulates acid secretion, impairs gastric motility, and damages the gastric mucosa
Caffeine in large amounts — stimulates gastric acid and can worsen epigastric burning, particularly in EPS
Spicy foods — common trigger for epigastric pain and burning in sensitive individuals
Carbonated drinks — increase intragastric gas and pressure, worsening bloating and fullness
Eating late at night — reduces gastric emptying time available before sleep, increasing nocturnal symptoms
Lying down immediately after eating — slows gastric emptying and increases reflux risk
Prolonged, unsupervised PPI use without reassessment — masks symptoms without addressing drivers, and carries its own risks with long-term use
Highly restrictive elimination diets without professional guidance — can lead to nutritional deficiency and food-related anxiety without reliably identifying triggers

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A normal endoscopy does not mean nothing is wrong — it means no structural damage was found. FD involves real biological dysfunction in motility, nerve sensitivity, and mucosal immune activity that endoscopy cannot see.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told 'everything looks normal' after invasive testing is often interpreted — by both patients and clinicians — as a signal that symptoms are exaggerated or psychological. This causes many people to stop investigating or to feel dismissed.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who understand that FD is a disorder of function rather than structure — like a software problem, not a hardware defect — are better positioned to pursue the right investigations (motility testing, duodenal biopsies, H. pylori testing) and the right treatments (neuromodulators, gut-brain therapies, prokinetics) rather than cycling through endoscopies waiting for a structural answer that will not come.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Stress and anxiety are not just consequences of FD — they are active biological drivers of it, mediated through the gut-brain axis. Addressing only the gut while ignoring mental health leaves a major lever untouched.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Many people rightly resist the framing that their symptoms are 'just stress', because it has historically been used to dismiss them without treatment. Acknowledging the gut-brain connection can feel like validating a dismissive narrative.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who frame gut-brain interventions (CBT, hypnotherapy, neuromodulators, mindfulness) as tools for retraining a sensitised nervous system — rather than admissions that symptoms are imaginary — tend to engage more effectively with these treatments and achieve meaningful, lasting reductions in symptom severity.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no universal FD treatment that works for most people — individual drivers vary significantly, and management requires systematic identification of personal triggers and mechanisms, not just following a standard protocol.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People naturally want a diagnosis followed by a definitive treatment. The idea that FD requires prolonged self-investigation and iterative trial-and-error is exhausting and frustrating, especially for those who have already been unwell for years.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who commit to methodical self-investigation — structured food diaries, systematic elimination, targeted testing for SIBO, H. pylori, gastroparesis, and coeliac disease — arrive at a clearer personal picture of their condition and achieve better outcomes than those waiting for a clinician to hand them a complete answer.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many gastroenterologists manage FD with PPIs or a single medication trial and consider the workup complete after a normal endoscopy. Finding the right clinician — one who investigates motility, mucosal immune function, and the gut-brain axis — often requires active effort from the patient.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People trust that the specialist system will route them to the right expertise. Accepting that it may not — and that advocating for specific tests or seeking a second opinion may be necessary — feels burdensome, particularly when already unwell.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who research the full diagnostic landscape of FD (gastric emptying studies, duodenal biopsy eosinophil counts, H. pylori testing, breath testing for SIBO), identify which tests they have not had, and specifically request them or seek out clinicians who routinely offer them — are more likely to identify actionable findings.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The only intervention in FD with genuinely curative potential for a subset of people. It is inexpensive, low-risk, and non-invasive. Missing this step means missing the only straightforward fix.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a urea breath test or stool antigen test from a GP or gastroenterologist. Ensure PPIs have been stopped for at least 2 weeks beforehand. If positive, complete the full eradication course and confirm eradication with a follow-up test.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks from treatment completion to symptom assessment

key_levers.why_it_matters: Visceral hypersensitivity and gut-brain dysregulation are central mechanisms in FD. Low-dose neuromodulators directly target these pathways, reduce pain thresholds, and in the case of mirtazapine, improve gastric accommodation. The DIAMOND trial data strongly supports amitriptyline for FD, yet it remains underused in clinical practice.
key_levers.how_to_start: Raise the option with a gastroenterologist or GP who is familiar with gut-brain pharmacology. Starting doses are very low (10 mg amitriptyline or nortriptyline at night) and titrated slowly. A 3-month trial is typically needed to assess benefit.

key_levers.lever: Dietary restructuring — meal size and fat reduction
key_levers.why_it_matters: In impaired gastric accommodation (the dominant mechanism in PDS), meal volume and fat content are the two most direct triggers for postprandial symptoms. Reducing them reduces the physiological demand on a dysfunctional system.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin a 2-week trial of 5–6 small meals per day with fat content at approximately 20–25% of calories. Track symptoms carefully. Work with a dietitian to ensure nutritional adequacy.
key_levers.timeline: 1–3 weeks to observe change in postprandial symptoms

key_levers.why_it_matters: Gut-brain therapies (hypnotherapy, CBT, MBSR) address the central sensitisation and autonomic dysregulation that perpetuate FD. They produce durable benefit rather than ongoing symptom suppression and complement — not replace — physical treatments.
key_levers.how_to_start: Look for a therapist with specific experience in functional GI disorders. The Monash University gut-directed hypnotherapy programme and the Rome Foundation directory are useful starting points. Self-guided audio programmes by Whorwell's group are an accessible entry point.
key_levers.timeline: 8–12 weeks of consistent practice for meaningful change

key_levers.why_it_matters: FD frequently co-occurs with SIBO, coeliac disease, EPI, gastroparesis, and MCAS — conditions that can be identified and specifically treated. Remaining on symptom-management medications indefinitely without investigating these drivers produces partial and unstable improvement.
key_levers.how_to_start: Create a list of conditions not yet tested for. Work through them systematically — starting with those that have non-invasive testing (H. pylori, coeliac serology, breath testing, thyroid function) before moving to more complex investigations.
key_levers.timeline: 3–12 months to complete a thorough workup and act on findings

common_mistakes: Accepting 'normal endoscopy = nothing wrong' as the end of the workup — functional tests (gastric emptying, accommodation, breath testing) and biopsy interpretation are separate and often not performed

Long-term PPI use without reassessment or investigation of the underlying driver — PPIs treat acid-related symptoms but do not address motility, visceral hypersensitivity, or duodenal mucosal inflammation

Pursuing ever more restrictive elimination diets without a structured reintroduction — this can lead to nutritional deficiency, disordered eating patterns, and social isolation without reliably identifying true triggers

Refusing neuromodulators because 'it's a gut problem, not a mental health problem' — gut-brain medications work primarily on enteric nerve sensitivity, not mood, and missing them means missing a well-evidenced treatment
Waiting for a clinician to initiate investigation of all contributing conditions rather than self-advocating for specific tests — most routine FD workups stop after endoscopy and H. pylori testing
Treating anxiety and GI symptoms in isolation rather than as an interconnected system — both improve more when treated together
Abandoning interventions (dietary changes, psychological therapies, low-dose medications) prematurely — FD responds slowly; many people stop trials before the therapeutic window has fully elapsed

specialists.type: Gastroenterologist (with interest in functional GI disorders)
specialists.role: Performs and interprets endoscopy with biopsies, orders motility studies including gastric emptying scintigraphy, manages H. pylori eradication, and prescribes neuromodulators and prokinetics.
specialists.notes: Not all gastroenterologists take FD beyond endoscopy. Those with a declared interest in functional GI disorders, neurogastroenterology, or gut-brain medicine tend to offer more thorough investigation and management. Seeking a second opinion from a specialist at an academic or teaching hospital is worth considering if initial management is limited to PPIs.

specialists.role: Guides structured dietary investigation including FODMAP elimination and reintroduction, small meal restructuring, and nutritional adequacy monitoring during restriction.
specialists.notes: General dietary advice for FD is often vague. A dietitian trained specifically in functional GI conditions (look for those affiliated with Monash University FODMAP training or similar programmes) will provide evidence-based, personalised guidance rather than generic 'avoid spicy food' advice.

specialists.role: Investigates root causes including gut dysbiosis, SIBO, food sensitivities, mast cell activation, and mucosal immune dysfunction that are often not assessed in standard gastroenterology care.
specialists.notes: Mainstream clinicians may be sceptical of some functional medicine approaches. Look for practitioners who use evidence-based testing (breath tests, comprehensive stool analysis, elimination-reintroduction protocols) and are transparent about the evidence base for their recommendations.

specialists.role: Delivers gut-directed hypnotherapy, cognitive behavioural therapy adapted for functional GI disorders, and mindfulness-based interventions targeting the gut-brain axis.
specialists.notes: This is a small subspecialty and access varies greatly by geography. The Rome Foundation maintains a patient resources directory that includes practitioners. In areas without access, self-guided programmes and therapists willing to learn GI-specific protocols may be an alternative.

specialists.role: First port of call for initial testing (H. pylori, coeliac serology, thyroid, ultrasound), referrals, and prescription of first-line medications. Can also request gastric emptying studies if familiar with the evidence.
specialists.notes: Many GPs have limited time to investigate FD thoroughly. Being specific about which tests are being requested — and bringing evidence summaries or printed Rome IV criteria to the appointment — can significantly improve the efficiency of primary care consultations.

patient_communities.name: r/functionaldyspepsia
patient_communities.notes: The most active dedicated FD community online. Contains detailed shared experience about investigations, medications tried (including neuromodulators, prokinetics, and herbals), dietary strategies, and navigating the medical system. Posts from people who have achieved significant improvement are particularly valuable.

patient_communities.name: r/GERD
patient_communities.notes: Many people with FD initially seek help in this community due to symptom overlap. Contains useful experience about PPI use, dietary management, and distinguishing GERD from functional symptoms, though FD is not always clearly distinguished from reflux disease.

patient_communities.name: r/ibs
patient_communities.notes: Relevant for those with FD-IBS overlap. Extensive community experience with FODMAP diets, SIBO investigation, and gut-brain treatments that are also applicable to FD management.

patient_communities.name: Functional Gut Clinic patient resources
patient_communities.notes: UK-based specialist centre with publicly available educational content on functional GI disorders including FD. Useful for understanding the clinical landscape and what specialist care should look like.

learning_resources.title: Rome IV Functional Gastrointestinal Disorders — Patient Information
learning_resources.description: The Rome Foundation sets the diagnostic criteria for functional GI disorders worldwide. Their patient-facing resources explain the Rome IV criteria for FD in accessible language and provide background on the gut-brain axis and functional GI mechanisms.

learning_resources.title: Functional Dyspepsia — A Review
learning_resources.author_or_source: Talley NJ, Ford AC — New England Journal of Medicine, 2015
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive, authoritative clinical review of functional dyspepsia covering epidemiology, pathophysiology, diagnosis, and treatment by two of the leading researchers in the field. Useful for those who want to understand the medical evidence base in depth before or during clinical encounters.

learning_resources.title: The Gut-Brain Connection (Monash University FODMAP blog)
learning_resources.description: The Monash FODMAP team publishes accessible, evidence-based articles on functional GI disorders, the FODMAP diet, the gut-brain axis, and related topics. Particularly useful for those considering dietary approaches to FD management.

learning_resources.title: Gut: The Inside Story of Our Body's Most Underrated Organ
learning_resources.description: An accessible and engaging overview of how the gut works — including the enteric nervous system, gut motility, and the gut-brain connection. Does not address FD specifically but provides essential conceptual background that helps people with FD understand what is actually happening in their bodies.

learning_resources.title: Functional Dyspepsia: Mechanisms and Therapy (Gastroenterology Clinics)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Camilleri M — Gastroenterology Clinics of North America
learning_resources.description: A detailed review of the mechanisms underlying FD including impaired accommodation, duodenal hypersensitivity, and gut-brain dysregulation, with an evidence-based overview of therapeutic options. Best suited to those who want to understand the science at a clinical level.

learning_resources.title: Effect of Amitriptyline and Escitalopram on Functional Dyspepsia (DIAMOND Trial)
learning_resources.description: The landmark randomised controlled trial demonstrating that low-dose amitriptyline significantly outperforms placebo and PPI for functional dyspepsia, particularly epigastric pain syndrome. Essential reading for anyone whose clinician has not discussed neuromodulators as an option.

learning_resources.title: About IBS — Patient Education Centre (IFFGD)
learning_resources.description: Although primarily focused on IBS, IFFGD's resources cover functional GI disorders broadly, including functional dyspepsia. The site has patient-friendly explanations of gut-brain treatments, dietary approaches, and how to navigate the medical system — highly relevant given the FD-IBS overlap.

sections.relevance_signal: **Pain or fullness in the upper stomach** (not lower), early satiety, or burning — without an ulcer or H. pylori.
